questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Evaluate the statements below:
(A) An emf can be induced by moving a conductor through a magnetic field.
(B) An emf can be induced by altering the magnetic field.
options: Both A and B are false.
Both A and B are true.
A is true, while B is false.
B is true, while A is false.
questions.solution:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, an induced emf is generated whenever there is a change in the magnetic flux linked with a circuit or a conductor.

The magnetic flux $\Phi = B A \cos \theta$ can be changed by altering the magnetic field $B$, changing the area $A$, or changing the angle $\theta$.

Moving a conductor through a magnetic field changes the area swept by the conductor, which induces a motional emf. Thus, statement A is true.

Altering the magnetic field with time also changes the magnetic flux, which induces an emf. Thus, statement B is true.

Therefore, both statements A and B are true.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting loop is being withdrawn from a magnetic field at a speed $v$, as depicted in the figure. Which of the four plots shown in the figure could represent the power delivered by the pulling agent as a function of the speed $v$?
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in the conducting loop is given by $\varepsilon = B l v$, where $l$ is the length of the side of the loop perpendicular to the velocity and $B$ is the magnetic field.

The induced current in the loop is $I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{B l v}{R}$, where $R$ is the resistance of the loop.

The magnetic force opposing the motion of the loop is $F = I l B = \left(\dfrac{B l v}{R}\right) l B = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R}$.

To pull the loop at a speed $v$, the pulling agent must apply an external force equal in magnitude to the magnetic force. The power delivered by the pulling agent is $P = F v = \left(\dfrac{B^2 l^2 v}{R}\right) v = \dfrac{B^2 l^2 v^2}{R}$.

This shows that the power $P$ is directly proportional to the square of the speed $v$, i.e., $P \propto v^2$.

The graph of $P$ versus $v$ is a parabola opening upwards. Among the given plots, curve b correctly represents this parabolic relationship.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a conducting loop is being withdrawn from a magnetic field at a speed $v$. Which of the four plots shown in the figure could represent the power delivered by the pulling agent as a function of the speed $v$?
questions.solution:
When a conducting loop is withdrawn from a uniform magnetic field, the motional emf induced in the loop is given by:

$\varepsilon = Blv$

where $B$ is the magnetic field, $l$ is the length of the side of the loop inside the magnetic field, and $v$ is the speed of the loop.

The induced current in the loop is:

$I = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{R} = \dfrac{Blv}{R}$

where $R$ is the electrical resistance of the loop.

The magnetic force acting on the current-carrying side of the loop opposes its motion (according to Lenz's law) and is given by:

$F = IlB = \left(\dfrac{Blv}{R}\right)lB = \dfrac{B^2l^2v}{R}$

To pull the loop at a speed $v$, the pulling agent must apply an external force equal in magnitude to the magnetic force. The power $P$ delivered by the pulling agent is:

$P = Fv = \left(\dfrac{B^2l^2v}{R}\right)v = \dfrac{B^2l^2v^2}{R}$

From this expression, we can see that the power $P$ is directly proportional to the square of the speed $v$ ($P \propto v^2$).

The graph of $P$ versus $v$ should be a parabola opening upwards. Among the given plots, curve b correctly represents this quadratic relationship.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the arrangement shown in the figure, the wire $AB$ slides on the fixed rails at a constant velocity. If the wire $AB$ is substituted with a semicircular wire, the magnitude of the induced current will
options: increase or decrease depending on whether the semicircle bulges towards the resistance or away from it
questions.solution:
The motional emf induced in a conductor moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by $E = Bvl_{\text{eff}}$, where $l_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective length of the conductor.

The effective length is the straight-line distance between the endpoints of the conductor. When the straight wire $AB$ is replaced by a semicircular wire connecting the same points $A$ and $B$, the effective length remains unchanged.

Thus, the induced emf $E$ remains the same. Since the external resistance is constant and the wires are assumed to be ideal, the total resistance of the circuit remains unchanged.

Therefore, the induced current $I = \dfrac{E}{R}$ remains the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small, conducting circular loop is positioned inside a long, current-carrying solenoid. The plane of the loop contains the axis of the solenoid. When the current in the solenoid is altered, the induced current in the loop is
options: clockwise or anticlockwise depending upon whether the resistance is increased or decreased
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside a long current-carrying solenoid is uniform and directed along its axis.

Since the plane of the small conducting circular loop contains the axis of the solenoid, the magnetic field lines are parallel to the plane of the loop.

The angle between the magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ and the area vector $\vec{A}$ of the loop is $90^{\circ}$.

The magnetic flux through the loop is $\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = BA \cos(90^{\circ}) = 0$.

When the current in the solenoid is altered, the magnitude of the magnetic field changes, but its direction remains parallel to the plane of the loop. Thus, the magnetic flux through the loop remains zero at all times.

Since there is no change in magnetic flux ($\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = 0$), the induced electromotive force (EMF) is zero.

Therefore, the induced current in the loop is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting square loop having side $l$ and resistance $R$ travels in its plane at a uniform velocity $v$ perpendicular to one of its sides. A steady and uniform magnetic field $B$ is present perpendicular to the plane of the loop, as shown in the figure. The induced current in the loop is
questions.solution:
The magnetic flux linked with the square loop is given by $\Phi = BA$, where $A = l^2$ is the area of the loop.

Since the magnetic field $B$ is uniform and steady, and the loop is moving completely inside this uniform field, the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the loop remains constant with time.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced emf is given by:
$e = -\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$

Since $\Phi$ is constant, $\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = 0$, which means the induced emf $e = 0$.

Consequently, the induced current in the loop is:
$I = \dfrac{e}{R} = 0$

Alternatively, using the concept of motional emf, the emf induced in the two vertical sides of the loop is $Blv$ each, with the same polarity (e.g., top end at a higher potential). These two emfs oppose each other in the closed loop, resulting in a net emf of zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two circular loops having equal radii are positioned coaxially at a certain separation. The first loop is cut, and a battery is introduced to drive a current through it. Due to the variation of resistance with temperature, the current experiences a slight change. During this interval, the two loops
options: attract or repel each other depending on the direction of the current
exert no force on each other
questions.solution:
When a battery is connected to the first loop, current flows through it, causing the wire to heat up due to the Joule heating effect.

For metallic conductors, resistance increases with an increase in temperature. As the resistance of the first loop increases, the current flowing through it decreases.

The decreasing current in the first loop leads to a decrease in the magnetic flux linked with the second coaxial loop.

According to Lenz's law, an induced current is generated in the second loop in such a direction as to oppose the decrease in magnetic flux. Therefore, the induced current in the second loop will flow in the same direction as the current in the first loop.

Since currents flowing in the same direction in parallel conductors attract each other, the two loops will attract each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rod of length $l$ revolves at a small, uniform angular velocity $\omega$ around its perpendicular bisector. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present parallel to the rotation axis. What is the potential difference between the rod's centre and one of its ends?
questions.solution:
Let the centre of the rod be the origin and the axis of rotation pass through it. The distance from the centre to one of the ends is $l/2$.

Consider a small element of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the centre. The velocity of this element is $v = \omega x$.

The motional emf induced in this small element is given by $dE = B v dx = B \omega x dx$.

The total potential difference between the centre and one end is obtained by integrating $dE$ from $x = 0$ to $x = l/2$:

$E = \int_{0}^{l/2} B \omega x dx$

$E = B \omega \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{l/2}$

$E = B \omega \left( \dfrac{(l/2)^2}{2} \right) = \dfrac{1}{8} B \omega l^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rod of length $l$ is rotating at a uniform angular velocity $\omega$ about its perpendicular bisector. A uniform magnetic field $B$ is present parallel to the rotation axis. The potential difference between the two ends of the rod is
questions.solution:
Let the center of the rod be $O$ and its two ends be $A$ and $B$.

The rod rotates about its perpendicular bisector, so the distance of both ends from the axis of rotation is $l/2$.

The motional emf induced between the center $O$ and end $A$ is given by:

$V_A - V_O = \dfrac{1}{2} B \omega \left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} B \omega l^2$

Similarly, the motional emf induced between the center $O$ and end $B$ is:

$V_B - V_O = \dfrac{1}{2} B \omega \left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} B \omega l^2$

The potential difference between the two ends $A$ and $B$ is:

$V_A - V_B = (V_A - V_O) - (V_B - V_O) = 0$

Alternatively, the induced emf across the ends can be calculated by integrating $dE = B v dx$ from $-l/2$ to $l/2$:

$E = \int_{-l/2}^{l/2} B (\omega x) dx = B \omega \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} \right]_{-l/2}^{l/2} = 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Observe the setup illustrated in the figure. When the switch is closed and subsequently opened after some time, the closed loop will exhibit
options: an anticlockwise current-pulse and then a clockwise current-pulse
a clockwise current-pulse and then an anticlockwise current-pulse
questions.solution:
When the switch is closed, current flows clockwise in the primary rectangular loop because the positive terminal of the battery (longer vertical line) is on the left.

The current in the right vertical wire of the primary loop is directed downwards. By the right-hand grip rule, this creates a magnetic field directed into the plane of the paper at the position of the adjacent circular loop.

As the switch is closed, the current increases from zero to a steady value, leading to an increase in the inward magnetic flux through the circular loop. According to Lenz's law, the induced current will oppose this increase by producing an outward magnetic field. By the right-hand grip rule, an outward magnetic field is generated by an anticlockwise current. Thus, an anticlockwise current pulse is induced.

When the switch is subsequently opened, the current in the primary loop decreases to zero. The inward magnetic flux through the circular loop decreases. To oppose this decrease, the induced current will produce an inward magnetic field, which corresponds to a clockwise current pulse.

Therefore, the closed loop will exhibit an anticlockwise current-pulse and then a clockwise current-pulse.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the situation shown in the figure. If the closed loop is completely enclosed in the circuit containing the switch, and the switch is closed and after some time it is opened again, the closed loop will show
options: an anticlockwise current-pulse and then a clockwise current-pulse
a clockwise current-pulse and then an anticlockwise current-pulse
questions.solution:
When the switch is closed, current flows from the positive terminal (longer line on the left) to the negative terminal (shorter line on the right) of the battery. This establishes a clockwise current in the rectangular circuit.

Using the right-hand thumb rule, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ produced by the downward current in the rightmost wire of the rectangular circuit is directed outwards (out of the plane of the paper) at the location of the small circular loop.

When the switch is just closed, the outward magnetic flux through the circular loop increases from zero. According to Lenz's law, the induced current in the circular loop will oppose this increase by creating an inward magnetic field. To produce an inward magnetic field, the induced current must be clockwise. Thus, a clockwise current-pulse is generated.

When the switch is opened, the current in the rectangular circuit drops to zero, causing the outward magnetic flux through the circular loop to decrease. According to Lenz's law, the induced current will oppose this decrease by creating an outward magnetic field. To produce an outward magnetic field, the induced current must be anticlockwise. Thus, an anticlockwise current-pulse is generated.

Therefore, the loop shows a clockwise current-pulse followed by an anticlockwise current-pulse.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet is released from rest along the central axis of a very long, vertical copper tube. After some time elapses, the magnet
options: will travel with a nearly constant speed
will come to rest inside the tube
will move with an acceleration equal to $g$
questions.solution:
When a bar magnet falls through a vertical copper tube, the magnetic flux linked with the tube changes continuously.

This changing magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the walls of the copper tube.

According to Lenz's law, the direction of these induced eddy currents is such that they oppose the downward motion of the magnet.

The upward opposing magnetic force is proportional to the velocity of the falling magnet. As the speed of the magnet increases, the opposing force also increases.

Eventually, the upward magnetic force exactly balances the downward gravitational force acting on the magnet.

At this point, the net force on the magnet becomes zero, and its acceleration becomes zero.

Thereafter, the magnet continues to fall with a nearly constant terminal speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The provided figure displays a horizontal solenoid connected to a battery and a switch. A copper ring is located on a frictionless track, with its axis aligned along the solenoid's axis. When the switch is closed, the ring will
options: move towards or away from the solenoid depending on which terminal (positive or negative) of the battery is attached to the left end of the solenoid
questions.solution:
When the switch is closed, the current in the solenoid increases from zero to a steady maximum value.

This increasing current produces an increasing magnetic field along the axis of the solenoid, which in turn increases the magnetic flux linked with the copper ring.

According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, an electromotive force (EMF) is induced in the ring. By Lenz's law, the direction of the induced current in the copper ring will be such that it opposes the change in magnetic flux that produced it.

To oppose the increasing magnetic flux, the induced current will create a magnetic field in the direction opposite to the solenoid's magnetic field. As a result, the face of the ring closer to the solenoid will develop the same magnetic polarity as the closer end of the solenoid.

Since like magnetic poles repel each other, the solenoid will exert a repulsive force on the copper ring. Therefore, the ring will move away from the solenoid, regardless of the polarity of the battery connections.
